title: Add HRP optimizer
description:
Add nanobook.optimize_hrp(returns) using hierarchical clustering on correlation + recursive bisection. Robust to ill-conditioned covariance matrices where Markowitz blows up.

**Technical Specification:**
optimize_hrp(returns: &[Vec<f64>]) -> OptimizerResult
- Compute correlation matrix from returns
- Convert to distance matrix: distance = sqrt(0.5 * (1 - correlation))
- Hierarchical clustering (single linkage or average linkage)
- Recursive bisection: split dendrogram into clusters, assign inverse variance weights
- Return OptimizerResult with weights, iters, converged, final_step_squared

Clustering options:
- Linkage method: Single, Average, Complete (configurable)
- Distance metric: 1 - correlation (standard for HRP)
- Cluster allocation: inverse variance weighting within each cluster

**Generalization Check:**
- Pure numerical optimization, no domain-specific assumptions
- Works with any returns matrix (not just equities)
- Generic portfolio construction applicable to any asset class
- No qtrade-specific data structures or assumptions

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Property tests: weights sum to ~1, all non-negative, cluster structure valid
- Property test: handles degenerate correlation matrices (perfect correlation)
- Reference parity: compare vs sklearn hierarchical clustering + López de Prado reference
- Edge cases: single asset, perfectly correlated assets, degenerate correlation matrix
- Numerical stability: handle ill-conditioned covariance matrices

**Implementation Guidance:**
- Extend optimize.rs with new optimize_hrp function
- May need hierarchical clustering implementation or dependency
- Consider scientific computing dependency (ndarray/linfa) or implement from scratch
- Start with simple linkage method (single) before complex methods
- Follow existing OptimizerResult patterns
- Python binding: py_optimize_hrp
- Add docstring with López de Prado (2016) reference

**Files:** src/optimize.rs (extend), python/src/lib.rs
**Dependencies:** May need scientific computing library for clustering (evaluate options)
close_reason: Successfully implemented. Added correlation_matrix, correlation_to_distance, single_linkage_clustering, recursive_bisection, optimize_hrp functions to src/optimize.rs (452 lines). No external dependencies, clustering from scratch. 14 new tests, all 283 tests pass. Python bindings added. Follows OptimizerResult patterns, López de Prado (2016) reference.

title: Add tear sheet result struct
description:
Add nanobook.TearSheet (or extend BacktestResult) with full reporting payload: monthly returns matrix, rolling Sharpe series, drawdown events, trade-level analytics. Data layer only, rendering belongs in consumers.

**Technical Specification:**
TearSheet struct (or extend BacktestResult):

- rolling_sharpe: Vec<f64> (e.g., 90-day rolling Sharpe)
- drawdown_events: Vec<DrawdownEvent> (from drawdown analytics)
- trade_analytics: TradeAnalytics { entry_exit_distribution, hold_time_histogram, win_loss_size_distribution }
- aggregate_metrics: Metrics (existing performance metrics)

TradeAnalytics details:
- entry_exit_distribution: Map<ExitReason, usize> (count trades by exit reason)
- hold_time_histogram: Vec<usize> (distribution of trade durations)
- win_loss_size_distribution: {win_sizes: Vec<f64>, loss_sizes: Vec<f64>} (P&L distribution)

**Generalization Check:**
- Data aggregation only, no rendering logic
- Works with any backtest result (not just qtrade strategies)
- Generic reporting structure applicable to any trading system
- No qtrade-specific visualization or formatting assumptions

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Property tests: monthly returns sum to total return, trade counts match
- Property test: rolling Sharpe computed correctly over windows
- Edge cases: single month, no trades, constant returns, short backtest
- Serializability: struct can be serialized to JSON/Parquet for consumer rendering

**Implementation Guidance:**
- Depends on bd-7vj (drawdown analytics) for DrawdownEvent
- Consider extending BacktestResult rather than new TearSheet struct
- Monthly returns: group returns by month/year, compute monthly P&L
- Rolling Sharpe: sliding window computation using existing Metrics
- Trade analytics: need trade-level data from backtest (may require BacktestResult extension)
- Python binding: py_tear_sheet or auto-populated field in BacktestResult
- Keep data-only — no HTML/plotting logic (consumer's responsibility)

**Files:** src/result.rs (extend BacktestResult) or new report.rs, src/portfolio/metrics.rs (for trade analytics), python/src/lib.rs

title: Phase 3.1: Tracing Infrastructure - Dependencies and subscriber setup
description:

## Objective

Configure JSON formatter for stdout and file appender for audit log separation. Add correlation ID generation for each rebalance run.

## Detailed Implementation

### Add Tracing Dependencies

Add to rebalancer/Cargo.toml:
- tracing (core tracing API)
- tracing-subscriber (subscriber implementations)
- tracing-appender (file logging)

### Replace env_logger with tracing-subscriber

In main.rs:

### Configure JSON Formatter

### Add Correlation ID Generation

## Dependencies

title: Phase 2.2: Two-Phase Kill Workflow - Graceful then forceful
description:
## Background and Context

Graceful shutdown may not work if the process is stuck or unresponsive. We need a two-phase kill workflow: try graceful shutdown first, then forceful cancellation if it times out.

## Objective

Implement two-phase kill workflow in the kill command. Phase 1: send SIGTERM, wait for graceful shutdown (30s timeout). Phase 2: if timeout, connect to broker directly and cancel orders.

## Detailed Implementation

### Phase 1: Graceful Shutdown

- Send SIGTERM to rebalancer process
- Wait for graceful shutdown (30s timeout)
- Write KillPhase1Started and KillPhase1Completed audit events
- Monitor process exit

### Phase 2: Forceful Cancellation

If Phase 1 times out:
- Write KillPhase2Started audit event
- Connect to broker directly (bypass the stuck process)
- Cancel all open orders at broker
- Write KillPhase2Completed audit event

### Audit Events

Add kill phase audit events:
- KillPhase1Started
- KillPhase1Completed
- KillPhase2Started
- KillPhase2Completed

### Timeout Configuration

Make timeout configurable (default 30s):
- Config option in config.toml
- Command-line flag override
- Environment variable support

## Success Criteria

- Two-phase workflow implemented
- Graceful shutdown attempted first with timeout
- Forceful cancellation if graceful times out
- Audit log captures both phases
- Integration tests verify both phases work

title: Phase 1.6B: Phased Rollout - Extend to positions and quotes
description:
## Background and Context

After validating write-ahead logging for orders (Phase 1A), we extend it to positions and quotes (Phase 1B). These are read-only operations but still need write-ahead logging for complete crash recovery coverage.

## Objective

Extend write-ahead logging to broker.positions() and broker.quotes() calls. Add intent logging before these calls and result logging after. Test and validate the extended coverage.

## Detailed Implementation

### Add Intent Logging for Positions

Add PositionsIntent checkpoint and log_positions_intent() function:

- Log intent before calling broker.positions()
- Log result after positions are fetched
- Recovery can detect incomplete position fetches
- Metadata: timestamp, target spec reference

### Add Intent Logging for Quotes

Add QuotesIntent checkpoint and log_quotes_intent() function:

- Log intent before calling broker.quotes()
- Log result after quotes are fetched
- Recovery can detect incomplete quote fetches
- Metadata: symbols requested, staleness threshold

### Update Recovery Logic

Extend recovery to handle PositionsIntent and QuotesIntent:

- Detect incomplete intents (intent without result)
- Query broker to reconcile if needed
- Update audit log with reconciliation results

### Testing

- Test position fetch write-ahead in dry-run mode
- Test quote fetch write-ahead in dry-run mode
- Verify recovery handles incomplete fetches
- Performance testing for additional logging overhead

### Validation

- Dry-run testing passes
- Staging testing validates broker reconciliation
- Production rollout with monitoring
- Performance degradation < 10%

## Success Criteria

- PositionsIntent and QuotesIntent checkpoints added
- Intent logging functions implemented
- Recovery handles incomplete position/quote fetches
- Testing passes for all scenarios
- Production rollout successful

title: Add walk-forward analysis primitive
description:
Add nanobook.walkforward(returns, params, n_windows, train_pct) for rolling re-fit with IS/OOS metrics per window. Pure numerical, fits execution scope.

**Technical Specification:**
walkforward(returns: &[Vec<f64>], params: &[Params], n_windows: usize, train_pct: f64) -> WalkforwardResult
- WalkforwardResult struct: { is_metrics: Vec<Metrics>, oos_metrics: Vec<Metrics>, window_boundaries: Vec<(usize, usize)> }
- Window logic: split data into n_windows, each window has train portion (train_pct) and test portion (1-train_pct)
- IS (in-sample): compute metrics on train portion using given params
- OOS (out-of-sample): compute metrics on test portion using params fitted on train portion
- Roll forward: each window's train portion includes previous test data (expanding window) or fixed window (configurable)

**Generalization Check:**
- Pure numerical optimization and validation
- Works with any parameter sweep and returns matrix
- No domain-specific assumptions about strategy type or asset class
- Generic robustness validation applicable to any model selection

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Property test: total OOS periods = expected count based on window configuration
- Property test: IS metrics computed on correct train periods
- Property test: OOS metrics computed on correct test periods
- Edge cases: n_windows=1, train_pct=0/1, empty returns, single window
- Parameter handling: params applied correctly to each window

**Implementation Guidance:**
- New WalkforwardResult struct in backtest_bridge.rs or new walkforward.rs
- Window splitting logic with configurable train/test split
- Reuse existing Metrics computation for IS/OOS periods
- Parameter sweep integration: params could be strategy hyperparameters or model parameters
- Python binding: py_walkforward with flexible parameter interface
- Consider integration with existing sweep functionality

**Files:** src/backtest_bridge.rs (extend) or new src/walkforward.rs, python/src/lib.rs

title: Add OHLC volatility estimators
description:
Add nanobook.realized_vol(open, high, low, close, method) with methods: close_to_close, parkinson, garman_klass, yang_zhang. Extract 5-7× more vol information per bar than close-to-close stdev.

**Technical Specification:**
realized_vol(open: &[f64], high: &[f64], low: &[f64], close: &[f64], method: VolMethod) -> Vec<f64>
- VolMethod enum: CloseToClose, Parkinson, GarmanKlass, YangZhang
- CloseToClose: standard deviation of close-to-close returns (baseline)
- Parkinson: sqrt((1/(4*n*ln2))) * sum(ln(high/low)^2) — uses high/low range only
- GarmanKlass: sqrt((1/n)) * sum(0.5*ln(high/low)^2 - (2*ln2-1)*ln(close/open)^2) — uses OHLC
- YangZhang: most complex, combines open-to-close and close-to-open variance, accounts for overnight gaps
- Output: Vec<f64> same length as input with NaN in lookback where applicable

**Generalization Check:**
- Pure statistical computation on OHLC data
- Works with any OHLC time series (not just price data)
- No domain-specific assumptions about asset class or market
- Generic volatility estimation applicable to any time series with OHLC

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Property test: output in valid range (non-negative, finite)
- Property test: OHLC consistency checks (high >= low, etc.)

title: Phase 2.3: Forceful Cancellation - Query broker, cancel all, verify with retry
description:
## Background and Context

When graceful shutdown fails or the process is stuck, we need forceful cancellation that bypasses the process entirely and cancels orders directly at the broker. This must be verified with retry logic to handle transient failures.

## Objective

Implement forceful cancellation: query broker for open orders, cancel all individually, verify cancellation with exponential backoff retry (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, max 5 retries).

## Detailed Implementation

### Broker Query for Open Orders

- Connect to broker (using existing connection logic)
- Query open_orders() to get all pending orders
- Handle connection failures with retry

### Cancel All Orders Individually

- Iterate through each open order
- Call broker.cancel_order() for each
- Track which cancellations succeed/fail
- Handle cancellation failures gracefully

### Verification with Retry

After cancellation:
- Wait 1s, query open_orders() again
- If orders remain, retry cancellation for remaining
- Repeat with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s)
- Max 5 retry attempts
- If still not empty after retries, log error with remaining order IDs

### Error Handling

- Log each cancellation attempt
- Log final state (success/partial/failure)
- Include remaining order IDs in error log
- Write KillForced event to audit log if forceful used

## Success Criteria

- Broker query for open orders implemented
- All orders cancelled individually
- Verification retry logic with exponential backoff
- Error logging for remaining orders
- Integration tests verify cancellation works
- Tests verify retry logic handles transient failures

title: Phase 2.1: Graceful Shutdown - SIGTERM handler and stop after current order
description:
## Background and Context

The current kill command sends SIGTERM but doesn't ensure the process stops safely. We need graceful shutdown logic that stops after the current order to avoid leaving partial orders at the broker.

## Objective

Implement SIGTERM handler in the rebalancer process with "stop after current order" logic. When SIGTERM is received, finish the current order submission if mid-flight, cancel remaining orders, and exit cleanly.

## Detailed Implementation

### SIGTERM Handler

Implement signal handler for SIGTERM:

- Use signal-hook crate for cross-platform signal handling
- Set a shutdown flag when SIGTERM is received
- Handle signal safely in async context

### Stop After Current Order Logic

Implement graceful shutdown flow:

1. Receive SIGTERM, set shutdown flag
2. If mid-order submission: wait for broker response
3. If between orders: stop immediately
4. Cancel all pending orders in queue
5. Write KillCompleted event to audit log
6. Exit cleanly

### Cancel Remaining Orders

After current order completes, cancel any remaining orders:

- Use existing broker.cancel_order() functionality
- Cancel all orders not yet submitted
- Wait for cancellation confirmation

### Audit Log Entry

Write graceful shutdown to audit log:

- KillCompleted event with method=graceful
- Include orders_cancelled_count
- Include duration_seconds

## Success Criteria

- SIGTERM handler implemented and tested
- Graceful shutdown stops after current order
- Remaining orders are cancelled before exit
- Audit log captures graceful shutdown
- Integration tests verify shutdown behavior

title: Phase 1.1: Audit Log Extensions - Add OrderIntent and OrderFailed checkpoints
description:
## Background and Context

This bead extends the audit log system to support write-ahead logging for crash recovery. The current audit log has checkpoints like OrderSubmitted and OrderFilled, but these are logged AFTER the broker call. If the process crashes between the broker call and the audit log write, we have a gap where the order exists at the broker but the audit log doesn't know about it. This can lead to duplicate orders on recovery.

## Objective

Add new checkpoint enum variants (OrderIntent, OrderFailed) to the audit log system to enable write-ahead logging. This allows us to log the INTENT to submit an order before calling the broker, then log the result after. If a crash happens, recovery can detect incomplete intents and reconcile with broker state.

## Detailed Implementation

### 1. Add OrderIntent Checkpoint

Add a new variant to the Checkpoint enum in rebalancer/src/audit.rs. The OrderIntent checkpoint is logged BEFORE calling the broker. It captures the full intent metadata so recovery knows what we intended to do.

### 2. Add OrderFailed Checkpoint

Add OrderFailed variant to capture failure reasons when broker calls fail. This includes error details so recovery can distinguish between known failures and ambiguous states.

### 3. Update Checkpoint Sequence Validation

The existing validate_checkpoints_from_parsed() function expects a specific sequence. We need to update it to handle the new checkpoint sequence where OrderIntent precedes OrderSubmitted or OrderFailed.

### 4. Add log_order_intent() Function

Create a new function to log order intent with full metadata including symbol, side, quantity, limit_price, client_order_id, timestamp, target spec reference, and execution context.

### 5. Add log_order_failed() Function

Create a new function to log order failure with error details including error type, error message, and context.

## Why This Matters

This is the foundation for write-ahead logging. Without OrderIntent, we can't detect incomplete submissions. Without OrderFailed, we can't distinguish between ambiguous failures and known failures. Both are needed for safe crash recovery.

## Considerations

- The checkpoint enum is serialized in the audit log, so adding variants is backward compatible via serde
- We need to update the checkpoint name mapping functions (as_event_name, from_event_name)
- The validation logic needs to handle the new optional checkpoint sequence
- Full intent metadata makes audit log entries larger, but the storage cost is negligible compared to safety benefits

## Success Criteria

- OrderIntent and OrderFailed variants added to Checkpoint enum
- as_event_name() and from_event_name() handle new variants
- validate_checkpoints_from_parsed() accepts new checkpoint sequence
- log_order_intent() function created with full metadata
- log_order_failed() function created with error details
- Unit tests verify checkpoint parsing and validation
- Existing audit log tests still pass (backward compatibility)

title: Phase 1.5: Testing - Crash injection and validation
description:
## Background and Context

Write-ahead logging is safety-critical code that prevents duplicate orders during crashes. We need comprehensive testing to verify it works correctly under all crash scenarios. This includes crash injection tests, golden fixture tests, and unit tests with mock brokers.

## Objective

Create comprehensive test suite for write-ahead logging including crash injection tests, golden fixture tests for audit log parsing, and unit tests for the wrapper function. Verify that crash recovery works correctly and no duplicate orders occur.

## Detailed Implementation

### Integration Test with Crash Injection

Create a test that intentionally crashes the rebalancer at specific points:

**Crash Points:**
1. After OrderIntent logging but before broker call
2. After broker call but before OrderSubmitted logging
3. During OrderSubmitted audit write
4. After OrderSubmitted but before next order

**Crash Injection Approach:**
- Use signal handling (SIGUSR1) to trigger controlled crash
- Or use special "crash_checkpoint" config value
- Test with real broker or high-fidelity mock broker

**Test Flow:**
1. Start rebalancer with crash injection enabled
2. Begin order submission sequence
3. Trigger crash at specific checkpoint
4. Run recovery mode
5. Verify reconstructed state matches broker state
6. Verify no duplicate orders
7. Verify audit log is valid and parseable

### Golden Fixture Tests

Create pre-crafted audit log files representing various scenarios:

**Fixture Files:**
- intent_only.jsonl: OrderIntent without OrderSubmitted/OrderFailed
- intent_success.jsonl: OrderIntent + OrderSubmitted (success path)
- intent_failure.jsonl: OrderIntent + OrderFailed (failure path)
- multiple_orders.jsonl: Multiple orders with mixed outcomes
- crash_recovery.jsonl: Realistic crash scenario

**Test Logic:**
1. Load fixture file into audit log
2. Run reconstruct_state()
3. Verify reconstructed state matches expected state
4. Verify determine_recovery_action() returns expected action
5. Test with and without write_ahead_logging feature flag

### Unit Tests for Wrapper Function

Create unit tests for execute_order_with_write_ahead() using mock broker:

**Test Cases:**
- Success path: broker call succeeds, verify intent and success logged
- Failure path: broker call fails, verify intent and failure logged
- Retry logic: transient failure then success, verify retry count
- Permanent failure: non-retryable error, verify no retry
- Timeout: broker timeout, verify error handling

**Mock Broker:**
Implement a mock broker that:
- Records all calls made to it
- Can simulate success/failure/timeout
- Returns configurable order_ids
- Tracks call count for retry verification

### Verification Tests

Add tests to verify the core safety properties:

**No Duplicate Orders Test:**
- Simulate crash after OrderIntent
- Recovery should detect ambiguous state
- Query broker should find the order
- Recovery should NOT resubmit (idempotency)

**Audit Log Validity Test:**
- Crash at various points
- Verify audit log is always valid JSON
- Verify checkpoint sequence is valid
- Verify audit log can be parsed completely

**Recovery Time Test:**
- Measure recovery time for typical crash scenarios
- Verify recovery completes in < 30 seconds
- Test with large order counts (100+ orders)

## Why This Matters

Testing is critical for safety-critical code. Write-ahead logging prevents duplicate orders, but only if it works correctly. Crash injection tests verify the system behaves correctly under real crash conditions. Golden fixture tests ensure audit log parsing is robust. Unit tests verify individual components work as expected.

## Considerations

- Crash injection tests need a controlled environment (test broker)
- Golden fixtures need to be updated when checkpoint format changes
- Mock broker should be high-fidelity to catch integration issues
- Tests should run in CI to prevent regressions
- Performance tests need to be realistic (not just toy examples)

## Success Criteria

- Integration test with crash injection passes for all crash points
- Golden fixture tests pass for all scenarios
- Unit tests for wrapper function cover all paths
- No duplicate orders in any crash scenario
- Audit log remains valid after crashes
- Recovery time < 30 seconds for typical crashes
- Tests run in CI without requiring external broker
- Feature flag tests verify both states work correctly

title: Phase 1.6A: Phased Rollout - Orders write-ahead only
description:
## Background and Context

Write-ahead logging for all state changes is a large change. To minimize risk, we're rolling it out in phases starting with the highest-value change: order submission only. This lets us validate the approach on the most critical path before extending to other operations.

## Objective

Implement and validate write-ahead logging for order submission only (Phase 1A). Test in dry-run mode first, then enable for live execution with monitoring. This is the first of three phased rollouts.

## Detailed Implementation

### Scope: Orders Only

For Phase 1A, implement write-ahead logging ONLY for order submission:

- OrderIntent before broker.execute_limit_order()
- OrderSubmitted/OrderFailed after broker call
- Recovery reconciliation for order intents
- NO write-ahead for positions, quotes, account summary, or cancellations yet

This limits the blast radius to the most critical operation (order submission) while allowing us to validate the core write-ahead pattern.

### Implementation Steps

1. **Complete Phase 1.1-1.5**: Ensure all order submission write-ahead components are complete
2. **Dry-run Testing**: Run rebalancer in dry-run mode with write_ahead_logging feature enabled
3. **Validation**: Verify audit log sequence is correct, no performance issues
4. **Staging Testing**: Enable in staging environment with test broker
5. **Production Dry-run**: Enable in production with dry-run mode (no real orders)
6. **Live Execution**: Enable for live execution with close monitoring
7. **Rollback Plan**: Be ready to disable feature flag if issues occur

### Dry-run Testing

Test with write_ahead_logging feature enabled in dry-run mode:

```bash
cargo build --package nanobook-rebalancer --features write_ahead_logging
rebalancer run target.json --dry-run
```

Verify:
- Audit log contains OrderIntent and OrderSubmitted events
- No errors or panics
- Performance is acceptable
- Audit log is parseable

### Staging Testing

Enable in staging environment with test broker:

- Use test IBKR account or mock broker
- Run full rebalance with real order flow
- Verify broker reconciliation works correctly
- Test crash scenarios in staging
- Monitor for any issues

### Production Rollout

**Step 1: Production Dry-run**
```bash
# Enable feature flag in production build
cargo build --package nanobook-rebalancer --features write_ahead_logging --release

# Run in dry-run mode first
rebalancer run target.json --dry-run
```

Monitor for:
- Audit log format changes
- Performance degradation
- Any errors or unexpected behavior

**Step 2: Live Execution with Monitoring**
After dry-run validation, enable for live execution:

- Enable feature flag in production
- Run first live rebalance with monitoring
- Watch for:
  - Order submission latency
  - Audit log write errors
  - Recovery scenarios
  - Any unexpected behavior

**Step 3: Monitor and Validate**
- Monitor for 1-2 weeks
- Check that crash recovery works if needed
- Verify no duplicate orders
- Ensure performance is acceptable

### Rollback Criteria

Be ready to disable feature flag if:
- Duplicate orders occur
- Performance degradation > 20%
- Audit log corruption or parsing errors
- Recovery failures
- Any unexpected behavior

Rollback is instant: rebuild without feature flag and redeploy.

## Why This Matters

Phased rollout minimizes risk by validating the approach incrementally. Order submission is the highest-value target (prevents duplicate orders), so we get immediate safety benefits while limiting the blast radius. If issues occur, we only need to roll back order submission changes, not a full system rewrite.

## Considerations

- Dry-run mode doesn't test broker reconciliation (no real broker state)
- Staging environment should be as production-like as possible
- Monitoring needs to be in place before live execution
- Rollback plan should be documented and tested
- Communication with stakeholders about the rollout

## Success Criteria

- Write-ahead logging works for order submission only
- Dry-run testing passes without issues
- Staging testing validates broker reconciliation
- Production dry-run passes monitoring checks
- Live execution enabled with no issues
- No duplicate orders in production
- Performance degradation < 10%
- Audit log remains valid and parseable
- Rollback plan tested and documented

title: Phase 1.4: Feature Flag - write_ahead_logging
description:
## Background and Context

Write-ahead logging is a significant change to the core execution flow. We need a feature flag to allow safe rollout and testing without affecting production. This allows us to develop and test the feature incrementally, and disable it instantly if issues occur.

## Objective

Add write_ahead_logging feature flag to rebalancer/Cargo.toml and gate all write-ahead logging logic behind this flag. This enables safe development, testing, and phased rollout of the crash recovery system.

## Detailed Implementation

### Add Feature Flag to Cargo.toml

Add to rebalancer/Cargo.toml:

```toml
[features]
write_ahead_logging = []  # Enable write-ahead logging for crash recovery
```

This feature flag is off by default during development.

### Gate Wrapper Function

In execution.rs, gate the execute_order_with_write_ahead() function:

```rust
#[cfg(feature = "write_ahead_logging")]
fn execute_order_with_write_ahead(...) -> Result<...> {
    // write-ahead implementation
}

#[cfg(not(feature = "write_ahead_logging"))]
fn execute_order_with_write_ahead(...) -> Result<...> {
    // fallback to direct broker call (original behavior)
    client.execute_limit_order(...)
}
```

Or use a runtime check if preferred:

```rust
if cfg!(feature = "write_ahead_logging") {
    // use write-ahead wrapper
} else {
    // use direct call
}
```

### Gate Recovery Reconciliation

In recovery.rs, gate the broker reconciliation logic for OrderIntent events:

```rust
#[cfg(feature = "write_ahead_logging")]
fn reconcile_order_intent(...) -> Result<...> {
    // broker reconciliation implementation
}
```

The recovery system should still parse OrderIntent events (for forward compatibility), but only attempt broker reconciliation when the feature is enabled.

### Update Tests

Ensure tests run with both feature flag states:

```bash
cargo test --package nanobook-rebalancer
cargo test --package nanobook-rebalancer --features write_ahead_logging
```

Add test variants that verify:
- Behavior is correct without the feature (backward compatibility)
- Behavior is correct with the feature enabled
- Audit logs are compatible across both modes

## Why This Matters

Feature flags are essential for safe rollout of high-risk changes. Write-ahead logging touches the core order submission path, so we need the ability to:
- Develop without affecting production
- Test in isolation
- Roll back instantly if issues occur
- Run A/B tests if needed
- Migrate gradually

## Considerations

- The feature flag should be compile-time, not runtime, for simplicity
- We need to ensure the audit log format is backward compatible
- Tests should cover both feature flag states
- Documentation should clearly explain how to enable the feature
- The feature flag name should be descriptive and not likely to conflict

## Success Criteria

- write_ahead_logging feature flag added to Cargo.toml
- execute_order_with_write_ahead() gated behind feature flag
- Recovery reconciliation logic gated behind feature flag
- Tests pass with both feature flag states
- Backward compatibility maintained (existing tests pass without feature)
- Documentation updated with feature flag usage

title: Phase 1.2: Write-Ahead Wrapper Function - execute_order_with_write_ahead()
description:
## Background and Context

Currently, order submission in execution.rs directly calls client.execute_limit_order() and logs the result after. This creates a gap where if the process crashes between the broker call and the audit log write, the order exists at the broker but the audit log doesn't know about it. We need a wrapper function that implements write-ahead logging: log intent before the call, log result after.

## Objective

Create a wrapper function execute_order_with_write_ahead() that implements the write-ahead logging pattern. This function encapsulates the entire order submission lifecycle with proper audit trail, making it crash-safe and recoverable.

## Detailed Implementation

### Function Signature

```rust
fn execute_order_with_write_ahead(
    broker: &mut impl Broker,
    audit: &mut AuditLog,
    order: &RebalanceOrder,
    client_order_id: &ClientOrderId,
    timeout: Duration,
) -> Result<OrderExecutionResult>
```

### Write-Ahead Flow

1. **Log OrderIntent**: Call log_order_intent() with full order metadata BEFORE calling the broker. This captures what we intend to do.

2. **Call Broker**: Execute broker.execute_limit_order() with the order parameters.

3. **Log Result**: 
   - On success: Call log_order_submitted() with the broker's order_id
   - On failure: Call log_order_failed() with error details
   - This creates the append-only audit trail

4. **Return Result**: Return the OrderExecutionResult to the caller

### Retry Logic with Exponential Backoff

Broker calls can fail transiently (network glitches, IBKR rate limits). Add retry logic:

- Initial attempt: immediate
- Retry 1: wait 1s
- Retry 2: wait 2s  
- Retry 3: wait 4s
- Retry 4: wait 8s
- Max 5 retries total

Only retry on transient errors (network timeout, connection reset). Don't retry on permanent errors (invalid symbol, insufficient margin).

### Success Path: OrderIntent → OrderSubmitted

When the broker call succeeds:
1. Log OrderIntent (already done)
2. Broker call succeeds, returns order_id
3. Log OrderSubmitted with broker order_id
4. Return success result

The audit log now has both the intent and the confirmation, creating a complete paper trail.

### Failure Path: OrderIntent → OrderFailed

When the broker call fails:
1. Log OrderIntent (already done)
2. Broker call fails with error
3. Log OrderFailed with error details (error type, message, context)
4. Return error result

Recovery can distinguish this from an ambiguous state - it knows the order definitely failed.

### Integration with Existing Code

Replace all direct calls to client.execute_limit_order() in the order submission loop with calls to execute_order_with_write_ahead(). The loop in execution.rs (lines 286-356) currently has:

```rust
match client.execute_limit_order(...) {
    Ok(result) => {
        audit::log_order_submitted(&mut audit, order, result.order_id)?;
        // ... handle result
    }
    Err(e) => {
        // ... handle error
    }
}
```

Replace with:

```rust
match execute_order_with_write_ahead(&mut client, &mut audit, order, &client_order_id, timeout) {
    Ok(result) => {
        // ... handle result (logging already done in wrapper)
    }
    Err(e) => {
        // ... handle error (logging already done in wrapper)
    }
}
```

## Why This Matters

This wrapper is the core of the write-ahead logging system. It ensures that every order submission has a complete audit trail that survives crashes. The retry logic handles transient failures without losing the audit trail. By encapsulating this in a wrapper, we keep the main execution flow clean and make the write-ahead logic testable in isolation.

## Considerations

- The wrapper should be feature-gated behind write_ahead_logging flag during development
- Retry logic needs to distinguish between transient and permanent errors
- The wrapper should not swallow errors - it should log them and propagate them
- Timeout handling needs to be preserved from the original call
- The wrapper must be thread-safe if we ever add parallel order submission

## Success Criteria

- execute_order_with_write_ahead() function created with correct signature
- Write-ahead flow implemented: intent → broker call → result logging
- Retry logic with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, max 5 retries)
- Success path: OrderIntent → OrderSubmitted with broker order_id
- Failure path: OrderIntent → OrderFailed with error details
- All direct client.execute_limit_order() calls replaced with wrapper
- Unit tests with mock broker verify all paths (success, failure, retry)
- Integration tests verify audit log sequence is correct
- Feature flag gates the wrapper during development

title: Add per-symbol attribution decomposition
description:
Add nanobook.decompose_backtest(weights, returns) returning per-symbol equity + trades. Pure linear math: contribution = weight × period_return, trade detection via weight transitions.

**Technical Specification:**
Input: weights: T×N matrix (T periods, N symbols), returns: T×N matrix (same shape)
Output: PerSymbolAttribution struct with:
- symbol_equity: N×T matrix (each symbol's cumulative equity over time)
- trades: Vec<TradeEvent> with entry/exit detection
- contribution: N×T matrix (weight × return per symbol per period)

Trade detection logic:
- Entry: weight transitions from 0 to non-zero between t-1 and t
- Exit: weight transitions from non-zero to 0 between t-1 and t  
- TradeEvent { symbol, entry_time, exit_time, entry_weight, exit_weight, hold_periods }

**Generalization Check:**
- Pure linear algebra, no domain-specific assumptions
- Works with any weight matrix and return matrix (not just equities)
- No qtrade-specific data structures or file formats
- Generic enough for portfolio attribution in any context

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Property test: sum of all symbol contributions = total portfolio return at each period
- Property test: symbol_equity sums to total equity at each period
- Edge cases: single symbol, constant weights, all-zero weights, missing data
- Trade detection: correct entry/exit timing, hold period calculation

**Implementation Guidance:**
- New struct PerSymbolAttribution in portfolio module
- New function decompose_backtest(weights: &[Vec<f64>], returns: &[Vec<f64>]) -> PerSymbolAttribution
- Use existing portfolio accounting patterns
- Python binding via py_decompose_backtest
- Consider integration with BacktestResult as opt-in field

**Files:** src/portfolio/mod.rs (or new src/portfolio/attribution.rs), python/src/lib.rs
close_reason: Successfully implemented. Created src/portfolio/attribution.rs (540 lines) with PerSymbolAttribution, TradeEvent, decompose_backtest function. Added comprehensive tests (16 unit tests, 23 integration tests, 8 Python tests). All tests pass (39 total new tests, no regressions). Python bindings added via py_decompose_backtest. Pure linear algebra, no domain-specific assumptions.

title: Phase 4.2: Startup Validation - Call validation at main() startup
description:
## Background and Context

Validation functions need to be called at startup to fail fast before any trading logic runs. We need to integrate validation into the main() function with actionable error messages and exit with non-zero status code on failure.

## Objective

Add validation call at start of main() before config loading. Implement actionable error messages with file/line references. Exit with non-zero status code on validation failure. Add --skip-validation flag for testing (not for production).

## Detailed Implementation

### Add Validation Call at Startup

In main.rs, add validation call before config loading:
- Call all validation functions
- Collect all validation errors
- If any errors, print and exit

### Implement Actionable Error Messages

Each validation error should include:
- Config file path
- Line number of the problematic value
- Current value
- Why it's invalid
- Suggested fix

Example:
```
Config validation failed:
  - risk.max_position_pct (150%) exceeds maximum (100%)
    Location: config.toml:42
    Fix: Set risk.max_position_pct to a value between 0 and 100
```

### Exit with Non-Zero Status Code

- Exit with status code 1 on validation failure
- Use different status codes for different error types if helpful
- Ensure exit code is non-zero for any validation failure

### Add --skip-validation Flag

- Add command-line flag to skip validation
- Only for testing purposes
- Document that this should not be used in production

## Dependencies

Depends on Phase 4.1 (validation functions).

## Success Criteria

- Validation call added to main()
- Actionable error messages implemented
- Exit with non-zero code on failure
- --skip-validation flag added
- Error messages include file/line references

title: Phase 1.3: Broker Reconciliation in Recovery - Handle OrderIntent events
description:
## Background and Context

The recovery system currently reconstructs state from audit logs but doesn't handle the new OrderIntent checkpoint. When recovery sees an OrderIntent without a corresponding OrderSubmitted or OrderFailed, it needs to reconcile with the broker to determine what actually happened. This is critical for crash recovery - we need to know if the order was actually submitted to the broker.

## Objective

Extend the recovery system to handle OrderIntent events by querying the broker to reconcile intent vs actual state. This allows recovery to distinguish between successful submissions, failed submissions, and ambiguous states that need broker verification.

## Detailed Implementation

### Extend compare_broker_state() for OrderIntent

The existing compare_broker_state() function compares broker state with reconstructed state from the audit log. We need to extend it to handle OrderIntent events:

1. **Detect Incomplete Intents**: Scan the recovered state for orders where:
   - OrderIntent checkpoint exists
   - Neither OrderSubmitted nor OrderFailed checkpoint exists
   - This indicates an ambiguous state - the order submission was interrupted

2. **Query Broker by client_order_id**: For each incomplete intent, query the broker's open orders to find orders with matching client_order_id. The client_order_id is our idempotency key that links the intent to the broker order.

3. **Reconciliation Logic**:
   - If broker has order with matching client_order_id: Update audit log with broker order_id, mark as OrderSubmitted
   - If broker has no matching order: Mark as OrderFailed, intent was never submitted
   - If broker query fails: Retry with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, max 5 attempts)

### Recovery State Reconstruction

Update reconstruct_state() to handle the new checkpoint sequence:

- When parsing OrderIntent: Create RecoveredOrder with submitted=false, filled=false
- When parsing OrderSubmitted: Update RecoveredOrder with submitted=true, broker order_id
- When parsing OrderFailed: Update RecoveredOrder with failure details

The reconstructed state now tracks three states per order:
- OrderIntent only: ambiguous (needs broker query)
- OrderIntent + OrderSubmitted: success
- OrderIntent + OrderFailed: known failure

### Recovery Actions

Add new recovery actions for incomplete intents:

1. **Query and Reconcile**: Connect to broker, query for orders with matching client_order_id, update audit log with findings
2. **Mark as Failed**: If broker has no matching order, mark intent as failed
3. **Manual Review**: If broker query fails after retries, require manual review

Update determine_recovery_action() to handle these new states:
- If all intents are resolved (success or failure): Safe to restart or resume
- If any intents remain ambiguous after broker query: Require manual review

### Broker Query Implementation

Implement the broker query logic:

```rust
fn reconcile_order_intent(
    broker: &dyn Broker,
    client_order_id: &ClientOrderId,
) -> Result<Option<BrokerOrderId>>
```

This function:
1. Queries broker.open_orders()
2. Searches for order with matching client_order_id
3. Returns Some(order_id) if found, None if not found
4. Retries on network failures with exponential backoff

### Update Audit Log with Reconciliation Results

When broker reconciliation succeeds, update the audit log with the findings:

- If order found at broker: Append OrderSubmitted event with broker order_id
- If order not found: Append OrderFailed event with "not_found_at_broker" reason

This makes the reconciliation permanent in the audit trail - future recoveries won't need to query the broker again for this order.

## Why This Matters

This is the recovery side of write-ahead logging. The OrderIntent tells us what we intended to do, but we need broker reconciliation to know what actually happened. Without this, recovery would have to guess whether incomplete intents succeeded or failed, which could lead to duplicate orders or missed cancellations.

## Considerations

- Broker queries can be slow, so we need to batch them when possible
- The broker might not support client_order_id queries (though IBKR does)
- Network failures during broker queries need retry logic
- The reconciliation should be idempotent - running it multiple times should be safe
- We need to handle the case where the broker is unavailable during recovery

## Success Criteria

- compare_broker_state() extended to handle OrderIntent events
- Broker query by client_order_id implemented with retry logic
- Recovery state reconstruction handles OrderIntent, OrderSubmitted, OrderFailed sequence
- determine_recovery_action() updated for incomplete intents
- Audit log updates with reconciliation results
- Integration tests verify broker reconciliation for all scenarios
- Unit tests verify retry logic with mock broker
- Recovery correctly resolves ambiguous states to success/failure

title: Phase 1.6C: Phased Rollout - All state changes
description:
## Background and Context

After validating write-ahead logging for orders, positions, and quotes (Phases 1A and 1B), we complete the coverage by adding it to all remaining state changes: account summary, order cancellation, and any other broker-affecting operations.

## Objective

Extend write-ahead logging to all remaining state changes to achieve complete crash recovery coverage. This includes account summary, order cancellation, and any other operations that affect broker state or influence rebalancing decisions.

## Detailed Implementation

### Add Intent Logging for Account Summary

Add AccountSummaryIntent checkpoint and log_account_summary_intent() function:

- Log intent before calling broker.account_summary()
- Log result after account summary is fetched
- Recovery can detect incomplete account summary fetches

### Add Intent Logging for Order Cancellation

Add CancelIntent checkpoint and log_cancel_intent() function:

- Log intent before calling broker.cancel_order()
- Log result after cancellation attempt
- Recovery can detect incomplete cancellations
- Critical for kill switch safety

### Review All Broker Calls

Audit rebalancer code for all broker calls:

- Identify any broker calls not yet covered
- Add intent logging for each
- Ensure no gaps in write-ahead coverage

### Update Recovery Logic

Extend recovery to handle all new intent types:

- Detect incomplete intents for all operations
- Query broker to reconcile if needed
- Update audit log with reconciliation results

### Final Testing and Validation

- Comprehensive testing of all write-ahead paths
- Crash injection tests for all operations
- Performance testing for total overhead
- Production rollout with monitoring

## Success Criteria

- All broker calls have write-ahead logging
- Recovery handles incomplete intents for all operations
- Comprehensive testing passes
- Performance degradation < 10%
- Production rollout successful
- Complete crash recovery coverage achieved

title: Phase 4.1: Validation Functions - Broker, risk limits, permissions, disk space
description:
## Background and Context

Configuration validation at startup prevents config errors from being discovered mid-execution. We need validation functions for broker connectivity, risk limits, file permissions, disk space, required fields, and network timeouts.

## Objective

Create validator.rs module in rebalancer/src/ with validation functions for all config areas. Implement broker connectivity check, risk limits validation, file permissions check, disk space check, required fields validation, and network timeout validation.

## Detailed Implementation

### Create validator.rs Module

Create rebalancer/src/validator.rs with validation functions.

### Broker Connectivity Check

- Test broker connection at startup
- Verify host:port is reachable
- Verify authentication works
- Return actionable error if connection fails

### Risk Limits Validation

- Validate risk limit values are within reasonable bounds
- Check max_position_pct is 0-100
- Check max_leverage is reasonable (e.g., 1-10)
- Check values are non-negative where required
- Return actionable errors with line numbers

### File Permissions Check

- Verify audit log directory is writable
- Check config file is readable
- Return actionable error if permissions are insufficient

### Disk Space Check

- Verify sufficient disk space for audit logs
- Check available space > threshold (e.g., 1GB)
- Return actionable error if disk space is low

### Required Fields Validation

- Verify all required config fields are present
- Check for missing critical values
- Return actionable error with field names

### Network Timeout Validation

- Verify connection timeout is reasonable (e.g., 5-60 seconds)
- Check timeout is not too short or too long
- Return actionable error if timeout is invalid

## Dependencies

None (can run in parallel with any phase).

## Success Criteria

- validator.rs module created
- All validation functions implemented
- Each validation returns actionable errors
- Unit tests for each validation function

title: Phase 3.2: Span Structure - Hybrid hierarchy run→phases→orders
description:
## Background and Context

With tracing infrastructure in place, we need to implement the hybrid span structure: rebalance_run (top-level) → phases (connect, fetch positions, etc.) → orders (per-order spans). This provides hierarchical context for debugging.

## Objective

Implement hybrid span structure with metadata at each level. Convert existing log statements to tracing macros. Add span context to error messages.

## Detailed Implementation

### Implement Span Hierarchy

Create span structure:
- rebalance_run (top-level): metadata (target file, account, cron mode, sequence number)
  - connect_to_broker: metadata (host, port, connection duration)
  - fetch_positions: metadata (symbol count, equity)
  - fetch_quotes: metadata (symbol count, staleness check results)
  - compute_diff: metadata (order count, notional value)
  - risk_check: metadata (risk checks passed/failed)
  - execute_orders: metadata (total orders)
    - submit_order (per-order): metadata (symbol, side, quantity, limit_price, client_order_id, duration, outcome)
  - reconcile: metadata (position deltas, final equity)

### Add Span Guards

Use #[span] attribute or span! macro:
- Add #[span] attribute to functions
- Or use span! macro for manual span creation
- Ensure proper span nesting

### Add Metadata to Spans

- Record relevant parameters as span fields
- Include timing information
- Add correlation ID to top-level span

### Convert Log Statements

Replace log::info!, log::warn!, log::error! with tracing::info!, tracing::warn!, tracing::error!
- Preserve log messages
- Add span context where helpful
- Ensure structured fields are consistent

### Add Span Context to Error Messages

- Include span context in error reporting
- Link errors to specific spans
- Help debugging by tracing error to span

## Dependencies

Depends on Phase 3.1 (tracing infrastructure).

## Success Criteria

- Hybrid span structure implemented
- Metadata added to each span level
- Log statements converted to tracing macros
- Span context added to error messages
- Span hierarchy matches execution flow

title: Add drawdown analytics
description:
Add nanobook.drawdown_series(equity) returning drawdown events with underwater duration, recovery time, peak/trough/recovery dates. Add rolling_max_drawdown(equity, window). Extend Metrics with max_underwater_days.

**Technical Specification:**
drawdown_series(equity: &[f64]) -> Vec<DrawdownEvent>
- DrawdownEvent struct: { peak_idx, trough_idx, recovery_idx (or None if not recovered), peak_value, trough_value, drawdown_pct, underwater_periods, recovery_periods (or None) }
- Peak detection: new high watermark
- Trough detection: lowest point after peak before recovery
- Recovery: when equity exceeds previous peak
- Underwater periods: trough_idx - peak_idx
- Recovery periods: recovery_idx - trough_idx (if recovered)

rolling_max_drawdown(equity: &[f64], window: usize) -> Vec<f64>
- Sliding window maximum drawdown computation
- Output same length as input with NaN in initial window

Metrics extension:
- Add field: max_underwater_days: usize (longest underwater streak across all drawdowns)

**Generalization Check:**
- Pure numerical analysis of equity curves
- Works with any equity curve (not just trading strategies)
- No domain-specific assumptions about what the equity represents
- Generic risk analytics applicable to any time series

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Property test: drawdown events are disjoint and chronologically ordered
- Property test: peak values are monotonically increasing across events
- Property test: recovery_idx > trough_idx when recovered
- Edge cases: monotonically increasing equity (no drawdowns), constant equity, single peak
- Rolling DD: window size handling, NaN in initial window

**Implementation Guidance:**
- New DrawdownEvent struct in portfolio/metrics.rs or new stats module
- drawdown_series: single pass peak/trough/recovery detection
- rolling_max_drawdown: sliding window with existing drawdown logic
- Extend Metrics struct with max_underwater_days field
- Python bindings: py_drawdown_series, py_rolling_max_drawdown
- Follow existing Metrics computation patterns

**Files:** src/portfolio/metrics.rs (extend), src/stats.rs (alternative), python/src/lib.rs
close_reason: Successfully implemented. Added DrawdownEvent struct, drawdown_series, rolling_max_drawdown functions to src/portfolio/metrics.rs. Extended Metrics with max_underwater_days field. Added Python bindings (py_drawdown_series, py_rolling_max_drawdown). 14 new tests, all 35 metrics tests pass. Pure numerical analysis, works with any equity curve.

title: Add López de Prado validation suite
description:
Add CPCV (Combinatorial Purged Cross-Validation), PBO (Probability of Backtest Overfitting), and Deflated Sharpe Ratio. These operate on sweep results matrices and are the canonical overfitting detection toolkit.

**Technical Specification:**
combinatorial_purged_cv(returns: &[Vec<f64>], n_splits: usize, n_test_groups: usize, embargo_pct: f64) -> CPCVResult
- CPCVResult: { is_metrics: Vec<Vec<Metrics>>, oos_metrics: Vec<Vec<Metrics>>, combinatorial_paths: Vec<Vec<usize>> }
- Generate N combinatorial train/test paths through the data
- Apply embargo (purge) period around train/test boundaries to avoid look-ahead
- Return IS/OOS metrics for each combinatorial path

pbo(is_metrics: &[Vec<f64>], oos_metrics: &[Vec<f64>]) -> PBOResult  
- PBOResult: { pbo_score: f64, p_value: f64, is_better: bool }
- Log-likelihood ratio test for overfitting
- Simulation-based p-value computation
- Returns probability that observed performance is due to luck

deflated_sharpe(sharpe: f64, n_trials: usize, skewness: f64, kurtosis: f64) -> f64
- Adjust Sharpe ratio for multiple testing and non-normality
- Uses skewness and kurtosis of returns distribution
- Returns deflated Sharpe that accounts for selection bias

**Generalization Check:**
- Pure statistical validation, no domain-specific assumptions
- Works with any sweep results matrix (not just trading strategies)
- Generic overfitting detection applicable to any model selection
- No qtrade-specific data structures or assumptions

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Property tests: output dimensions match expected, probabilities in [0,1]
- Reference parity: compare vs mlfinlab or known implementations
- Edge cases: small sample sizes, degenerate inputs, single trial
- Numerical stability: handle edge cases in log-likelihood computation

**Implementation Guidance:**
- Consider separate validation.rs module or extend stats.rs
- May need statistical dependencies or implement from scratch
- CPCV is most complex — consider feature gate or separate phase
- Start with Deflated Sharpe (simplest) first
- Python bindings with clear statistical documentation
- Add references to López de Prado (2018) in docstrings

**Files:** src/stats.rs (extend) or new src/validation.rs, python/src/lib.rs
**Note:** High complexity, consider implementing in phases (Deflated Sharpe first, then PBO, then CPCV)
close_reason: Successfully implemented Phase 1 (Deflated Sharpe). Added deflated_sharpe function to src/stats.rs with standard normal CDF/inverse CDF helpers. 6 new tests, all 274 tests pass. Python bindings added. PBO and CPCV deferred to future phases per bead specification. López de Prado (2018) reference in docstrings.

title: Phase 3.4: Testing - JSON format, correlation IDs, span hierarchy, performance
description:
## Background and Context

Structured logging is a significant change to the logging infrastructure. We need comprehensive testing to verify JSON format is parseable, correlation IDs are consistent, span hierarchy is correct, and performance overhead is minimal.

## Objective

Create comprehensive tests for observability: verify JSON log format is parseable, correlation IDs are consistent across spans, span hierarchy matches execution flow, test log output in various scenarios, and performance test to ensure tracing doesn't add significant overhead.

## Detailed Implementation

### JSON Format Tests

- Verify log output is valid JSON
- Test with jq or JSON parser
- Ensure no malformed entries
- Test with various log levels

### Correlation ID Tests

- Verify correlation ID is generated for each run
- Verify correlation ID appears in all log entries for that run
- Verify correlation IDs are unique across runs
- Test correlation ID propagation through span hierarchy

### Span Hierarchy Tests

- Verify span hierarchy matches execution flow
- Test parent-child span relationships
- Verify span metadata is correct
- Test span nesting depth

### Scenario Tests

Test log output in various scenarios:
- Successful rebalance run
- Failed rebalance (risk check failure)
- Partial order fill
- Network timeout
- Crash scenario

### Performance Tests

- Measure latency overhead of tracing
- Compare with original log crate performance
- Verify overhead < 5%
- Test with high log volume

## Dependencies

Depends on Phase 3.3 (migration) since testing needs the final logging implementation.

## Success Criteria

- JSON format tests pass
- Correlation ID tests pass
- Span hierarchy tests pass
- Scenario tests pass
- Performance overhead < 5%
- Tests run in CI

title: Phase 3.3: Migration - Convert log statements to tracing macros
description:
## Background and Context

After implementing span structure, we need to migrate all existing log statements from the log crate to tracing macros. This ensures consistent structured logging throughout the codebase.

## Objective

Convert all log::info!, log::warn!, log::error! calls to tracing::info!, tracing::warn!, tracing::error!. Add span guards where appropriate. Update log format to include span context. Test that log output is valid JSON.

## Detailed Implementation

### Convert Log Macros

Search and replace across codebase:
- log::info! → tracing::info!
- log::warn! → tracing::warn!
- log::error! → tracing::error!
- log::debug! → tracing::debug! (if any)

### Add Span Guards

Add #[span] attributes to functions:
- Identify functions that represent logical operations
- Add #[span(skip(args))] for functions that shouldn't record arguments
- Or use span! macro for manual span creation

### Update Log Format

- Ensure logs include span context automatically
- Verify structured fields are consistent
- Add correlation IDs where appropriate

### Test JSON Output

- Verify log output is valid JSON
- Test with jq or other JSON parser
- Ensure no malformed log entries

## Dependencies

Depends on Phase 3.2 (span structure) since conversion should happen after spans are defined.

## Success Criteria

- All log statements converted to tracing macros
- Span guards added where appropriate
- Log format includes span context
- Log output is valid JSON
- Existing functionality preserved

title: v0.13 F6: TWS restart mid-position (reconnect drill)
description:
Process survives TWS restart. Reconnect logic queries IBKR's open-positions, reconciles against audit log, resumes monitoring. Test target: reconcile within 30s of TWS coming back up.

DEEPENED PLAN (see plan_failure_modes_deepening.md):

Phase 1: Disconnect Detection (2-3 days)
- Add heartbeat mechanism to IBKR client (ping/pong via EWrapper)
- Detect heartbeat timeout (10s without response)
- Track connection state: Connected, Disconnected, Reconnecting
- Implement auto-reconnect with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s max)

Phase 2: Open Positions Query (2-3 days)
- Add reqAllOpenOrders() and openOrder() EWrapper methods
- Add reqPositions() and position() EWrapper methods
- Implement reconcile_state() method to query broker state
- Build local order and position caches

Phase 3: State Reconciliation (3-4 days)
- Define reconciliation invariants (order ID uniqueness, position consistency, fill consistency)
- Implement reconciliation algorithm: compare local vs broker state, detect orphans/missing fills
- Add reconciliation safety checks: block orders on failure, manual review for orphans
- Log all reconciliation discrepancies

Phase 4: Reconnect Drill Test (2 days)
- Extend MockTws with simulate_disconnect() and simulate_reconnect()
- Implement F6 integration test: submit order, fill partially, inject disconnect, reconnect, verify reconciliation
- Add 30s target measurement and performance assertion

TOTAL EFFORT: ~9-12 days

RISKS: TWS API rate limits, complex reconciliation logic, 30s target achievability
close_reason: done
